message.content:
Dynamic-link Library (DLL) Injection is a process injection technique where adversaries inject DLLs into processes to evade defenses and potentially elevate privileges. This method involves writing the DLL path into the target process's virtual address space and loading it via a new thread, often using Windows API calls like VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, and CreateRemoteThread. Variations include reflective DLL injection and DLL hollowing, which help conceal malicious code and bypass address relocation issues.

The impact of DLL injection includes unauthorized access to process memory, system/network resources, and elevated privileges, while also evading detection by security products. Common exploitation methods involve using legitimate software to execute malicious DLLs, reflective DLL loading, and injecting into processes like browsers to mask network connections.

Mitigation strategies include employing application whitelisting, monitoring API calls related to process injection, using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect anomalous behavior, and implementing strict access controls to limit the ability to write to process memory. Regularly updating and patching software can also reduce the risk of exploitation through known vulnerabilities.
